learn.title: Hydrogen Fuel Cells in Vehicles
learn.intro: Hydrogen fuel cells are transforming automotive energy by producing electricity cleanly as cars move. They combine hydrogen gas with oxygen, emitting only water vapor. This technology promises zero tailpipe emissions, high efficiency, and quick refueling—key for reducing transport’s carbon footprint and achieving net‑zero goals.
learn.c1_text: Think of a fuel cell as a tiny battery that keeps charging. Hydrogen enters the anode, splits into protons and electrons. Electrons flow through an external circuit to power the motor, while protons travel to the cathode to combine with oxygen, forming water. The process is like a continuous spark that never stops.
learn.c2_text: Fuel cells convert about 60–70% of hydrogen’s energy into electricity, compared to 20–30% for internal combustion engines. The rest is lost as heat. This high efficiency means a 1‑kg hydrogen pack can power a car for roughly 300 km, similar to a 200‑kWh battery.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Hydrogen Mission aims to produce 5 GW of green hydrogen by 2030, with incentives for domestic fuel cell vehicle production. Pilot projects in Delhi and Bengaluru are testing 1‑MW fuel cell buses, showcasing scalability for Indian cities.
learn.stat4: India target: 5 GW green hydrogen by 2030

learn.title: Wind Turbine Life & Recycling
learn.intro: Wind turbines are the backbone of renewable electricity, yet their blades reach the end of life after 20–25 years. Understanding lifespan, maintenance, and recycling is essential to keep wind farms green and to recover valuable materials like carbon fiber and aluminum, closing the loop in the energy transition.
learn.c1_text: Modern turbines last about 25 years. Blades endure constant wind stress, leading to micro‑cracks. Regular inspections every 6 months and real‑time vibration monitoring help spot wear before catastrophic failure, ensuring safety and reliability.
learn.c2_text: Routine tasks include pitch‑control checks, gearbox lubrication, and blade surface cleaning. Predictive maintenance using AI can cut downtime by 30%, saving operators millions while maintaining grid stability.
learn.c3_text: India’s wind sector is expanding, with 70 GW installed by 2030. Blade recycling plants in Gujarat aim to recover 35% of fiber and 20% of aluminum, reducing landfill waste and supporting a circular economy.

learn.title: India EV Policy Overview
learn.intro: India’s electric vehicle (EV) policy is a mix of national schemes like PM e‑DRIVE and state‑level incentives, creating a supportive environment for EV adoption. These policies drive lower emissions, reduce fuel dependence, and stimulate domestic manufacturing, propelling India toward a cleaner transport sector.
learn.c1_text: Launched in 2022, PM e‑DRIVE offers up to ₹1.5 lakh per vehicle for EV purchases. It also funds charging infrastructure in cities, aiming to install 10,000 charging points by 2025, making EV ownership affordable and convenient.
learn.c2_text: States like Karnataka and Maharashtra offer tax rebates, free parking, and subsidized charging. Karnataka’s 2023 policy reduced EV purchase tax from 13% to 3%, boosting sales by 25% in a year.
learn.c3_text: India’s EV sales grew 40% in 2023, reaching 300,000 units. The government’s goal is 30% EV share in passenger vehicles by 2030, aligning with the National Action Plan on Climate Change.
learn.stat2: PM e‑DRIVE subsidy: ₹1.5 lakh per vehicle

learn.title: National Green Hydrogen Mission
learn.intro: India’s National Green Hydrogen Mission is a strategic push to position the country as a global green hydrogen leader. By incentivizing renewable‑powered electrolysis and building a domestic supply chain, the mission aims to cut costs, create jobs, and support decarbonization across industry and transport.
learn.c1_text: The mission targets 5 GW of green hydrogen production by 2030, with a cost goal of ₹6–8 per kg. It will boost domestic manufacturing of electrolyzers and fuel cells, creating 100,000 jobs in the green tech sector.
learn.c2_text: Incentives include tax breaks, subsidies for renewable power, and a 20% discount on capital costs for green hydrogen projects. The government also offers a 10‑year loan at 4% for large‑scale electrolyzers.
learn.c3_text: India already operates 0.5 GW of green hydrogen plants, with plans to scale to 5 GW by 2030. The country’s abundant solar and wind resources position it to become a major exporter of green hydrogen to Europe and the Middle East.

learn.title: Small Wind Turbines for Homes
learn.intro: Small wind turbines bring the power of the wind into homes and communities, offering a reliable, low‑noise renewable energy source. They can offset grid electricity, provide backup power, and enable local energy independence, making them a practical step toward sustainable living.
learn.c1_text: A small turbine is like a mini windmill: the rotor spins in wind, driving a generator that produces AC electricity. Typical units range from 400 W to 10 kW, enough to power lights, chargers, or a small kitchen appliance.
learn.c2_text: Optimal placement is 10–15 m above ground, away from obstructions. A 10 kW turbine can generate ~3.5 MWh annually in a windy site, covering 10–15% of an average household’s electricity needs.
learn.c3_text: India’s rural electrification projects use 1–3 kW turbines to power schools and health centers. The government’s 2024 policy offers a 20% subsidy on small‑wind kits, boosting adoption in remote villages.
learn.stat1: India: 1–3 kW turbines in rural projects (2024)

learn.title: Understanding Capacity Factor
learn.intro: Capacity factor explains why a power plant’s rated power differs from its actual output over time. It is a key metric for comparing renewable assets, planning grid stability, and optimizing investment decisions—critical for scaling clean energy worldwide.
learn.c1_text: Capacity factor = actual energy produced ÷ (rated power × time). For a 1 MW plant running all day, a 100% factor means 24 MWh/day. Most solar farms hit 20–25%, while wind plants achieve 35–45% due to variable wind speeds.
learn.c2_text: Higher capacity factors mean more electricity from the same investment, reducing levelized cost of electricity (LCOE). For example, a 5 MW wind farm with a 40% factor yields 17.5 MWh/day, versus 12.5 MWh/day at 30%.
learn.c3_text: India’s wind farms average 30% capacity factor, while solar parks average 18%. Improving grid integration and storage can raise these numbers, helping India reach its 450 GW renewable target by 2030.
